html:
Wind and Straight-Line Storm Damage on Ark-La-Tex Roofs

Shreveport-Bossier doesn't sit on the coast, but the roofs here still take a beating from wind. Fast-moving squall lines roll through on short notice most springs and early summers, some of them tornado-warned, and by the time a storm has passed, a flat commercial roof can have lifted membrane, missing coping, and flashing pulled loose in places that aren't obvious from the ground.

We're a roofing contractor, not a public adjuster. When wind damage hits a building we work with, our job is to inspect the roof, document exactly what the wind did to it, and produce a scope that addresses the real extent of the damage, including the edge and perimeter work that often gets missed on a quick look. This is one piece of our broader roof insurance claim documentation work, applied to the specific way wind damages a flat roof.

How Wind Actually Damages a Flat Commercial Roof

Wind rarely tears a membrane apart in the middle of a roof field. It works at the edges first, at parapet corners, coping joints, and perimeter fastening patterns, because that's where uplift pressure concentrates during a fast-moving storm. Once wind gets under a membrane edge, it can peel back sections that were otherwise intact, which is why edge and corner detailing gets close attention during our post-storm walk.

  • Membrane lifted or torn loose along the perimeter or at roof corners
  • Coping and edge metal blown off or left partially detached
  • Flashing separated at parapet walls from repeated flutter during sustained wind
  • Rooftop equipment shifted or curbs cracked from wind-driven movement
  • Debris punctures from tree limbs or loose material carried onto the roof

Some years, the remnants of Gulf hurricanes push inland along the I-20 and I-49 corridors, bringing sustained wind and heavy rain well after the storm has weakened from its coastal strength. That combination, wind plus a large volume of rain hitting a roof already under stress, is a different kind of event than a fast squall line, and it tends to show up differently in drainage and ponding as much as in torn membrane.

Documenting Wind Damage for an Adjuster

Wind damage documentation has to connect specific damage to a specific event, since the same edge or corner can also show ordinary wear that has nothing to do with the storm. We date every photo, note wind direction and roof orientation where it's relevant to the damage pattern, and separate storm-caused damage from pre-existing wear rather than lumping everything into one line item. If you need that documentation for your own records or your adjuster's review, our documentation support puts the full package together as part of the same inspection.

Where the roof needs it, we also note code-required upgrades tied to wind resistance, like updated fastening patterns or edge metal specifications, so the repair scope reflects current requirements rather than just replacing what was there before with the same detail that failed.

Getting the Building Stable First

If wind has opened the roof to active water intrusion, dry-in comes before documentation, not after. Once the building is stable, we move to a full assessment and build the scope from there. A roof left under a temporary patch for an extended stretch is one more storm away from a second round of damage, so we move through that sequence with some urgency once the building is safe. For the full picture on what happens if the wind punched through the membrane, our emergency tarp and dry-in page covers that response step in detail, and our storm damage repair service covers the reroof or recover work once the claim scope is set.

Wind Damage Across Different Roof Types on the Same Building

A lot of the commercial roofs in Shreveport-Bossier carry more than one roof system, especially casino and hospitality properties along the riverfront that have grown through additions and renovations over the years. A straight-line wind event can lift membrane at the perimeter of a newer TPO section while leaving an older gravel-surfaced built-up section untouched a few feet away, which is why we walk the entire roof rather than assuming one storm produces the same damage pattern everywhere it passed. Warehouse and manufacturing roofs off I-20 tend to be more uniform in system but larger in field area, so wind damage there is more about how far it spread across the membrane than about mixed systems reacting differently.

Questions About Wind and Storm Roof Claims

Does wind damage always show up right after the storm?

Not always. Lifted seams and loosened edge metal sometimes hold through the initial event and fail during the next wind, which is part of why a prompt post-storm inspection matters even if nothing looks obviously wrong from below.

How do you tell wind damage from normal roof aging?

Wind damage typically shows fresh separation at edges, corners, and flashing points tied to a specific event, while aging shows up gradually across the field of the roof over years. We look at both the pattern and the timing to make that distinction.

Do you handle our insurance claim for wind damage?

No. We document and substantiate what the wind did to the roof so you and your adjuster have an accurate scope to work from, but the claim itself stays between you and your carrier.

Can a wind-damaged roof be repaired without a full reroof?

Often, yes, if the damage is limited to specific areas like edge metal, flashing, or isolated membrane sections. If the damage is widespread on a roof already near the end of its service life, we'll tell you that plainly.

Do hurricane remnants cause real damage this far inland?

They can, mostly through sustained wind and heavy rain overwhelming drainage that's fine for ordinary rainfall but not a multi-inch event. It's a different damage pattern than a fast squall line and gets documented differently.
